name: Epoch-Based Reclamation (EBR)
summary: Keir Fraser's EBR (2003): a global epoch counter advances when all threads in critical sections have observed the current epoch; objects retired in epoch e freed once all threads reach e+2. Library-encapsulated, transparent to data-structure code. Cheap readers (one acquire load + relaxed store per pin) but unbounded retention under stalls. Crossbeam (Rust) adapts EBR to the affine type system, making safety a borrow-check obligation.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Keir Fraser's **EBR** (Cambridge thesis, 2003): a global epoch counter advances when all threads in critical sections have observed the current epoch; objects retired in epoch *e* are freed once all threads reach *e+2*. Library-encapsulated and transparent to data-structure code — readers simply pin/unpin around access without naming the specific pointers being protected.

Only three epoch values are needed (mod-3 invariant). Batched deferred destruction amortises the scan across many retires.

sections.title: Cost model and trade-offs
sections.content:
Cost: reader one acquire load + relaxed store per pin; SeqCst fence on first pin to publish. Substantially cheaper per-access than hazard pointers, which is the main reason EBR dominates in practice for general-purpose lock-free libraries.

Worst-case retention: unbounded — a single stalled or preempted thread holding a pin freezes reclamation globally. This is the canonical EBR weakness and the reason most schemes from Hazard Eras onward (`data/hazard-eras`, `data/interval-based-reclamation`, `data/hyaline-reclamation`, `data/wait-free-eras`, `data/vbr-reclamation`, `data/nbr-reclamation`) try to combine EBR-grade throughput with HP-grade bounds.

In practice EBR is fine because preemption stalls are rare and short, but for hard-real-time or adversarial-scheduling systems the bound matters and HP/IBR/Hyaline are preferred.

sections.title: Crossbeam — EBR in Rust
sections.content:
**Crossbeam** (Rust) adapts EBR to Rust's affine type system: a `Guard` RAII pin plus `Atomic<T>` / `Shared<'g, T>` whose lifetime is bounded by the guard, statically preventing use-after-pin. Lifetime-parameterised shared pointers convert reclamation safety into a borrow-check obligation — the same machinery that prevents dangling references in safe Rust now prevents premature reclamation in lock-free code.

**GhostCell** composes with crossbeam to give zero-cost branded interior mutability for graph-shaped lock-free structures: a brand `'id` token authorises mutation through any cell carrying that brand, avoiding per-field locks while remaining safe.

Crossbeam is the foundation of most Rust lock-free libraries: `crossbeam-deque`, `crossbeam-queue`, `crossbeam-skiplist`, `flurry` (Java ConcurrentHashMap port), and the work-stealing schedulers in Tokio and Rayon all build on crossbeam-epoch.
